entities.name: veda
entities.cite: The Veda is the means of reaching Brahman

entities.name: śākhā
entities.cite: The plurality of sakhas is phenomenal

axioms.name: upaya
axioms.cite: The Veda is the means of reaching Brahman

axioms.name: anukara
axioms.cite: it is also anukara, a likeness or imitation of it

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: The plurality of sakhas is phenomenal, like the plurality of saktis

denials.name: parinamate
denials.cite: The plurality of sakhas is phenomenal, like the plurality of saktis

rejected_readings.label: veda_as_instructions_only
rejected_readings.rendering: The Veda serves only as an instrument to reach Brahman, not as Brahman itself.
rejected_readings.why: Treats veda as purely instrumental by degrading Brahman from absolute reality (word-essence) to a mere external goal (property). This severs the anukara (likeness) dimension; the commentary insists the Veda IS 'the goal in linguistic image', not merely a pointer to it. The claim upaya(veda, brahman-as-goal) is unlicensed: axioms sanction such relations only to brahman-as-absolute.

rejected_readings.label: sakhas_as_real_plurality
rejected_readings.rendering: The Veda genuinely exists as many distinct recensions rather than one appearing as many.
rejected_readings.why: Treats sakhas as satya (real multiplicity), contradicting the commentary's explicit claim that their plurality is phenomenal, like the plurality of saktis.

notes: The verse's core tension lies in upaya and anukara—two apparently distinct roles (means and likeness). The commentary resolves this through a non-dual reading: the Veda as a means to Brahman is continuous with attaining it because the Veda is Brahman's linguistic image. The sakhas parallel the saktis: both appear as multiplicities but are phenomenal manifestations of a unitary underlying reality.
